source: WHO Primary Care Guidelines + Kenya National Clinical Guidelines (2022)

conditions.name: Common Cold (Upper Respiratory Tract Infection)
conditions.description: A viral infection of the upper airways that typically resolves within 7–10 days.
home_care_tips: Rest and drink plenty of fluids (water, warm soup)
Use paracetamol for fever and headache relief
Gargle warm salty water for sore throat
Use saline nasal drops to relieve congestion
See a doctor if fever exceeds 39°C or symptoms worsen after 7 days

conditions.name: Influenza (Flu)
conditions.description: A more severe viral respiratory illness than the common cold, with sudden onset of systemic symptoms.
home_care_tips: Rest completely and isolate from vulnerable people
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and body aches
Drink plenty of fluids to prevent dehydration
Seek medical care if breathing becomes difficult or fever lasts more than 5 days

conditions.name: Malaria
conditions.description: A life-threatening parasitic disease transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes, highly prevalent in Kenya.
home_care_tips: Seek medical diagnosis immediately – a rapid malaria test (RDT) is required
Do not self-medicate with antimalarials without a confirmed diagnosis
Take prescribed antimalarials exactly as directed
Use treated mosquito nets and repellents to prevent reinfection
Return to hospital if symptoms worsen or you are unable to take oral medication

conditions.name: Typhoid Fever
conditions.description: A bacterial infection caused by Salmonella typhi, often contracted through contaminated water or food.
home_care_tips: Seek immediate blood or stool culture test for confirmation
Take prescribed antibiotics for the full course
Drink only boiled or treated water
Eat soft, easily digestible foods
Maintain strict hand hygiene to prevent spread

conditions.name: Gastroenteritis (Stomach Flu / Food Poisoning)
conditions.description: Inflammation of the stomach and intestines, typically caused by viral or bacterial infection.
home_care_tips: Drink ORS (oral rehydration salts) to replace lost fluids and electrolytes
Eat bland foods: rice, bananas, toast, boiled potatoes
Avoid dairy, fatty or spicy foods until recovered
Seek medical care if diarrhoea contains blood or lasts more than 3 days
Seek emergency care if signs of severe dehydration appear

conditions.name: Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
conditions.description: A bacterial infection of the urinary system, more common in women.
home_care_tips: See a doctor for urine culture and antibiotic prescription
Drink plenty of water to flush the urinary tract
Avoid holding urine for long periods
Wipe front-to-back after using the toilet
Seek emergency care if fever, back pain, or shivering develops (possible kidney infection)

conditions.name: Hypertension (High Blood Pressure) – Symptom-Related
conditions.description: Elevated blood pressure can cause these symptoms when uncontrolled. Regular monitoring is essential.
home_care_tips: Measure your blood pressure with a home device or at a pharmacy
Take prescribed antihypertensive medications consistently
Reduce salt intake to less than 5g per day
Exercise for at least 30 minutes most days of the week
Avoid smoking and limit alcohol
See your doctor if BP is above 140/90 mmHg consistently

conditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes – Symptomatic Episode
conditions.description: These symptoms may indicate undiagnosed or poorly controlled Type 2 Diabetes.
home_care_tips: See a doctor for fasting blood glucose or HbA1c test
Reduce intake of sugary drinks and refined carbohydrates
Increase physical activity to at least 150 minutes per week
If already diagnosed, check blood glucose levels and contact your care team
Inspect feet daily for wounds or sores that do not heal

conditions.name: Tension Headache
conditions.description: The most common type of headache, often triggered by stress, dehydration, or poor posture.
home_care_tips: Take paracetamol (500–1000mg) for pain relief
Apply a warm or cold compress to the neck and forehead
Drink a large glass of water – dehydration is a common trigger
Rest in a quiet, dark room
Practice deep breathing or gentle neck stretches
See a doctor if headaches are severe, frequent, or accompanied by vision changes

conditions.name: Anaemia
conditions.description: Low haemoglobin in the blood, often caused by iron deficiency, malnutrition, or chronic disease.
home_care_tips: See a doctor for a full blood count (FBC) to confirm
Eat iron-rich foods: beans, dark leafy greens, red meat, fortified cereals
Consume vitamin C with iron-rich foods to enhance absorption
Follow prescribed iron supplementation if directed
Avoid tea or coffee immediately after meals as they reduce iron absorption

conditions.name: Skin Infection / Cellulitis
conditions.description: A bacterial skin infection that can spread rapidly if untreated.
home_care_tips: See a doctor for antibiotic treatment
Keep the affected area clean and covered
Do not squeeze or drain pus at home
Elevate the affected limb if swollen
Seek emergency care if red streaks extend from the wound or fever develops

conditions.name: Anxiety
conditions.description: Anxiety is a common mental health response to stress, presenting with both physical and emotional symptoms.
home_care_tips: Practice 4-7-8 breathing: inhale 4 sec, hold 7 sec, exhale 8 sec
Limit caffeine and alcohol intake
Maintain a regular sleep schedule
Engage in physical exercise for 30 minutes daily
Talk to a trusted friend, family member, or counsellor
See a doctor if anxiety significantly disrupts your daily life

conditions.name: Back Pain (Non-specific)
conditions.description: Non-specific lower back pain is extremely common and often caused by muscle strain or poor posture.
home_care_tips: Apply warm compress for muscle relaxation
Take ibuprofen or paracetamol for pain relief
Stay active – gentle walking is beneficial
Avoid prolonged bed rest
See a doctor if pain radiates to the leg, causes numbness, or is associated with bladder problems

conditions.name: Asthma – Mild Episode
conditions.description: Asthma is a chronic respiratory condition that can flare up with triggers such as dust, smoke, or exercise.
home_care_tips: Use your prescribed reliever inhaler (salbutamol) immediately
Sit upright and remain calm
Identify and avoid known triggers
Seek emergency care if reliever does not work after 10 minutes
Follow up with your doctor to review your long-term control medication
